title: Sausage casserole
subtitle: Sausages braised with butter beans, tomato, and rosemary.
excerpt: Good pork sausages browned in a wide pan, then braised with tinned tomatoes, butter beans, rosemary, and a little smoked paprika until the sauce is thick and the sausages yield completely. A weeknight one-pot with no fuss.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional British sausage casserole practice and mid-twentieth-century domestic cookery. Tomato-based sausage braises are documented across British post-war cooking from the 1950s onwards, when tinned tomatoes became widely available in UK supermarkets. The butter-bean version is standard in contemporary British home cooking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions for up to three months. Defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat gently in a wide pan, adding a splash of water if the sauce has tightened.
recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple the recipe in a larger casserole. The technique doesn't change; allow a few more minutes for the sauce to reduce at the end.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Made the day before and reheated, the flavour deepens. The sausages hold their texture well overnight in the sauce.

content.text: The quality of the sausages matters here because they are the centre of the dish. A good butcher's pork sausage with a high meat content will release proper fat and flavour into the sauce; a cheap supermarket sausage produces a pale, oily puddle. Cumberland sausages, with their coarse grind and light spicing, work particularly well. Lincolnshire sausages, sage-forward, give the dish a slightly different character.

The entire recipe happens in one pan. Brown the sausages properly in the fat they release — pale sausages added to a braise taste boiled. The browning takes time and needs the pan on medium-high heat with enough space for each sausage to sit flat. Once browned, the tomatoes, beans, and aromatics go in and the heat drops. The casserole needs about 25 minutes of gentle simmering, uncovered at the end to let the sauce tighten.
Use a wide, heavy casserole or sauté pan that can hold all eight sausages in a single layer. A pan that is too narrow forces the sausages to steam against each other and brown unevenly.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide casserole over medium-high heat. Add {{sausages-pork}} sausages and cook, turning occasionally, until deeply browned on all sides, about 8 to 10 minutes. They don't need to be cooked through at this stage; they'll finish in the sauce. Remove and set aside.

Leave the fat in the pan. Reduce the heat to medium and add {{onion}} finely sliced onion. Cook for 6 to 8 minutes until soft and beginning to colour at the edges. Add {{garlic}} of finely sliced garlic, {{rosemary}} of chopped rosemary leaves, and {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika. Stir for one minute.

Pour in {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, crushing them as they go in. Add {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock and {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce. Stir and bring to a steady simmer. Add {{butter-beans}} of drained butter beans. Nestle the browned sausages back into the pan.

Simmer gently for 20 minutes with the lid on. Remove the lid and simmer for a further 10 minutes until the sauce has thickened to a consistency that coats the back of a spoon. Season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} of sea salt flakes and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Scatter with {{parsley-flat}} of flat-leaf parsley if using. Serve in shallow bowls with crusty bread or mashed potato.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: A straightforward braise with few failure points.

items.symptom: Sausages pale and soft, not browned
items.cause: Pan too crowded, or heat too low.
items.fix: Cook in batches if needed. The pan needs medium-high heat and space between each sausage.

items.cause: Too much stock, or not enough uncovered simmering time.
items.fix: Simmer uncovered at the end until the sauce reaches a coating consistency. It will tighten significantly in the last ten minutes.

items.cause: Tomatoes acidic, or smoked paprika too old and turned slightly bitter.
items.fix: Add a small pinch of granulated sugar to balance. Use fresh smoked paprika — it has a short shelf life once opened.

items.cause: Heat too high at the browning stage, or pricked before cooking.
items.fix: Brown on medium-high with steady turning, not violent flipping. Never prick sausages before cooking — the fat inside is what keeps them moist.

content.text: Sausage casserole is the midweek workhorse of British home cooking, the one-pot that asks little and delivers consistently. Its roots are in frugal post-war British cooking, when tinned tomatoes arrived in British kitchens in quantity and the one-pot braise became a domestic staple. The butter-bean version is the most satisfying: the beans thicken the sauce slightly as they cook and absorb the flavour of the sausage fat. Serve with thick mashed potato or dense white bread to catch the sauce.
